content.text: Cloud computing delivers computing resources — servers, storage, databases, networking, software, and analytics — over the internet on a pay-as-you-go basis. Rather than purchasing, installing, and operating physical hardware, organizations rent capacity from a provider's shared, globally distributed infrastructure and pay only for what they consume. The model fundamentally changes the economics of IT: what was once a large upfront capital investment becomes a variable operating expense that scales with actual usage.
What is Cloud Computing

The authoritative formal definition comes from the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), published in Special Publication 800-145: "Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction."

This definition contains several critical ideas. The resources are shared across many customers through multi-tenancy, meaning a single physical server might host dozens of isolated virtual machines belonging to different organizations. Access is available from any network-connected device anywhere in the world. Provisioning happens through self-service interfaces — no human at the provider needs to rack a server or configure a switch — and capacity can be released just as quickly as it was acquired. Consumption is metered and billed, typically by the second or hour, so users pay only for what they actually use.
The NIST Definition

NIST SP 800-145 identifies five essential characteristics that together distinguish cloud computing from conventional dedicated hosting or managed services. All five must be present for something to qualify as cloud computing under this definition.
The Five Essential Characteristics
On-demand self-service.

A consumer can provision compute resources — such as server time and network storage — unilaterally, automatically, and without requiring human interaction with the service provider. In AWS this manifests as the ability to launch a new EC2 instance or create an S3 bucket through the console, CLI, or API in seconds, at any time, with no phone call or ticket to AWS support.
Broad network access.

Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms that promote use by heterogeneous thin or thick client platforms — mobile phones, tablets, laptops, and workstations. Cloud services are accessible from any device with an internet connection using standard protocols (HTTPS, SSH, TLS), regardless of the operating system or device type.
Resource pooling.

The provider's computing resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model, with different physical and virtual resources dynamically assigned and reassigned according to consumer demand. Consumers generally have no control over or knowledge of the exact location of the provided resources, though they may be able to specify location at a higher level of abstraction (e.g., country, region, or data center). AWS operates pools of compute, storage, and networking capacity across dozens of Availability Zones worldwide.
Rapid elasticity.

Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released, in some cases automatically, to scale rapidly outward and inward commensurate with demand. To the consumer, the capabilities available for provisioning often appear to be unlimited and can be appropriated in any quantity at any time. An EC2 Auto Scaling group, for example, can launch hundreds of additional instances within minutes when CPU utilization rises and terminate them when demand falls — all without human intervention.
Measured service.

Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging a metering capability at some level of abstraction appropriate to the type of service (e.g., storage, processing, bandwidth). Resource usage can be monitored, controlled, and reported, providing transparency for both the provider and consumer. AWS bills for compute by the second, storage by the gigabyte-month, and data transfer by the gigabyte — all visible in real time through AWS Cost Explorer and CloudWatch.
Why it Matters

For most of computing history, organizations that needed compute capacity faced a painful choice: buy more servers than average demand required (to handle peaks) and waste money during quiet periods, or buy exactly what average demand required and fail under peak load. Server procurement took weeks to months. Hardware depreciated over 3–5 years, locking in decisions made before requirements were fully understood. A startup that guessed wrong about traffic growth could run out of capacity on launch day, or overspend on idle hardware if adoption was slower than expected.

Cloud computing dissolves these constraints. Capacity can be acquired in minutes and released just as quickly. A startup with zero assets can access the same global infrastructure as the world's largest enterprises. A media company can serve a global sports broadcast to millions of concurrent viewers and scale back 90 minutes later — without having invested in a single physical server. An e-commerce retailer can provision ten times normal capacity for a Black Friday event and pay only for the hours those extra instances run.

The second major impact is the shift from capital expenditure (CapEx) to operational expenditure (OpEx). Buying servers requires capital that must be reserved, depreciated on the balance sheet, and written off when the hardware ages out. Cloud services are billed monthly like a utility — they appear in the operating budget alongside electricity and rent. This makes IT spending visible, variable, and directly tied to business activity rather than to long-term hardware bets.
Benefits
No upfront capital cost.
Organizations avoid purchasing servers, networking gear, and data center space. Resources are expensed as consumed.
Instant global reach.
AWS operates in 30+ geographic regions. An application can be deployed across multiple continents in the same time it would take to rack a single server in an on-premises data center.
Managed services reduce operational burden.
Using Amazon RDS instead of self-hosting a database means AWS handles patching, backups, replication, and failover. Teams focus on application logic rather than infrastructure maintenance.
Speed of experimentation.
New ideas can be tested on real infrastructure in hours without procurement cycles. Failed experiments are deleted and stop incurring cost immediately.
Elasticity aligned with demand.
Workloads with predictable daily cycles, seasonal spikes, or sudden viral growth scale automatically rather than hitting a hardware ceiling.
Built-in reliability features.

AWS data centers are engineered with redundant power, cooling, and networking. Services like S3 are designed for 99.999999999% (eleven nines) durability by replicating data across multiple Availability Zones.
Trade-offs
Ongoing costs can exceed on-premises at scale.

Pay-as-you-go pricing is economical for variable workloads, but a large, stable, predictable workload running 24/7 may cost more in cloud than equivalent owned hardware over a 3-year period. This break-even analysis (sometimes called the cloud repatriation point) requires careful modeling.
Vendor lock-in risk.
Deeply adopting AWS-specific services (DynamoDB, Kinesis, Lambda) creates dependency on AWS APIs and pricing. Migrating away later is expensive and disruptive.
Network dependency.

Cloud workloads require a reliable internet or dedicated connection. Latency between the application and the cloud data center affects user experience, particularly for latency-sensitive applications.
Data sovereignty and compliance complexity.

Some regulations require data to remain within specific geographic boundaries or on infrastructure under direct organizational control. While AWS offers region selection and compliance certifications, verifying residency and demonstrating auditability requires deliberate design.
Shared tenancy risks.

Multi-tenant environments can experience "noisy neighbor" effects where other customers' workloads impact available IOPS or network bandwidth on shared physical hardware. AWS mitigates this with dedicated instances and placement groups, but these cost more.
Reduced infrastructure control.

Organizations cannot access the physical servers, customize firmware, or choose specific hardware generations without using specialized offerings (Dedicated Hosts, Outposts). Hypervisor and hardware choices are made by the provider.
How it Works

Cloud computing is built on virtualization. A hypervisor — software running directly on physical server hardware — creates and manages multiple isolated virtual machines (VMs) on a single physical host. Each VM believes it has exclusive access to CPU, memory, disk, and network, but these are actually carved out of shared physical resources by the hypervisor. AWS uses the Nitro hypervisor, a custom lightweight system designed for security and performance. This virtualization layer is what enables multi-tenancy: hundreds of customer workloads can run simultaneously on the same physical rack with strong isolation between them.

Above the hypervisor, a control plane exposes all resources as APIs. AWS's control plane accepts requests ("launch this EC2 instance", "create this S3 bucket") and fulfills them by scheduling work onto physical hardware, configuring networking, and returning resource identifiers to the caller. This API-first design is what enables on-demand self-service: there is no human in the provisioning loop. Consumers interact with the control plane through the AWS Management Console (a web UI), the AWS CLI, AWS SDKs, or Infrastructure-as-Code tools like CloudFormation and CDK.

Metering is implemented at the hypervisor and control plane level. Every CPU-second, every byte of storage, and every network packet is counted. This telemetry flows into AWS's billing system, which produces usage records that appear in Cost Explorer and on monthly invoices. The metering is sufficiently fine-grained that EC2 charges by the second (with a 60-second minimum) and Lambda charges by the 1ms invocation duration increment.
Architecture

The three cloud service models differ in how the management responsibility is divided between the provider and the customer. The diagram below shows which technology layers each party manages across On-Premises, IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS deployments.

attrs.alt: Cloud service models shared responsibility diagram showing which layers are managed by the customer versus the provider in On-Premises, IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS

content.text: On-premises, the organization manages every layer from physical hardware through the application. With IaaS the provider manages hardware, networking, storage, and virtualization; the customer controls the operating system and everything above it. PaaS extends provider management through the OS, runtime, and middleware, leaving only application code and data under customer control. In SaaS, the provider manages the entire stack — the customer interacts only with the application through a browser or API client. All three models run on the same shared physical data center infrastructure owned by the cloud provider.
Service Models
Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)

IaaS provides the fundamental building blocks of a data center as a service: compute (virtual machines), storage (block volumes, object storage), and networking (virtual private clouds, load balancers, DNS). The customer is responsible for the operating system, patching, runtime, middleware, and application. IaaS is the most flexible model and the closest to owning your own hardware — you have root access to the virtual machine and can install any software you need.

Primary AWS IaaS services: EC2 (virtual machines), EBS (block storage), S3 (object storage), VPC (virtual networking), ELB (load balancing), and Route 53 (DNS). IaaS is appropriate when the team requires full OS-level control, is running software that cannot be containerized or managed by a PaaS platform, or is migrating existing on-premises workloads with minimal modification.
Platform as a Service (PaaS)

PaaS abstracts away the operating system, runtime, and middleware. Developers deploy application code and configuration; the platform provisions, patches, and scales the underlying infrastructure automatically. The customer is responsible only for the application and data, while the provider manages everything beneath.

AWS PaaS examples: Elastic Beanstalk (deploy web apps without managing EC2 directly), AWS Lambda (run code without managing any servers at all — the extreme form of PaaS known as Function as a Service or FaaS), Amazon RDS (managed relational databases), and Amazon ECS/EKS (managed container orchestration). PaaS is appropriate when the team wants to focus on application development rather than infrastructure operations, and the application's requirements fit within the constraints of the platform.
Software as a Service (SaaS)

SaaS delivers a complete, ready-to-use application over the internet. The provider manages the entire stack including the application itself. Customers configure the application and interact with it through a web browser or API client, but do not install, patch, or scale any software. Common enterprise examples include email (Gmail, Microsoft 365), CRM (Salesforce), and collaboration tools (Slack, Zoom). AWS offers SaaS products such as Amazon WorkMail (email), Amazon Chime (video conferencing), and Amazon QuickSight (BI dashboards).
Deployment Models
NIST defines four cloud deployment models based on who owns the infrastructure and who has access to it:
Public cloud.

Infrastructure is owned and operated by a third-party cloud provider and made available to the general public. Multiple unrelated organizations share the same physical hardware (with logical isolation). AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud are public clouds. Resources are provisioned on-demand with no long-term commitment required.
Private cloud.

Infrastructure is provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization, which may own and operate it on-premises or have a dedicated provider host it. Private clouds offer the control and isolation of on-premises hardware with some cloud-style operational practices (self-service, automation). They address strict data residency or regulatory requirements.
Hybrid cloud.

An organization uses both private (on-premises) and public cloud infrastructure, connected by secure links (VPN, AWS Direct Connect). Sensitive workloads remain on-premises while burst capacity, disaster recovery, or new services run in the public cloud. AWS Outposts extends AWS infrastructure and services to customer data centers, enabling consistent hybrid operations.
Multi-cloud.

An organization uses services from two or more public cloud providers simultaneously — for example, AWS for compute and ML services alongside Google Cloud for data analytics. Multi-cloud avoids dependence on a single provider's availability or pricing, but introduces operational complexity from managing multiple toolchains and billing systems.
When to Use It

Cloud computing is the right choice in most scenarios for organizations that do not already have significant, fully utilized data center infrastructure. The economic and operational advantages are overwhelming for workloads with variable demand, new projects, or teams that cannot afford dedicated infrastructure operations staff.
Cloud is especially well-suited for:
Workloads with variable, unpredictable, or seasonal demand (e-commerce peaks, media launches, SaaS user growth)
New products and services where demand is unknown and overprovision carries real financial risk
Development, testing, and staging environments that run only during business hours and can be stopped overnight
Disaster recovery and backup targets where capacity sits idle most of the time but must be available instantly
Applications requiring global presence without the cost of establishing physical presence in multiple regions
Machine learning workloads that require GPU clusters for training but only sporadically
When Not to Use Cloud
Workloads that run at maximum capacity 24/7 for years — at sustained high utilization, owned hardware often costs less over 3–5 years than cloud billing
Environments with strict data residency or air-gap requirements that legally prohibit storing data on third-party infrastructure
Applications requiring sub-millisecond latency where network round-trip to a cloud region is unacceptable (edge computing or on-premises processing is preferable)
How to Implement

The entry point for AWS is configuring the AWS CLI with credentials from an IAM user or role. Best practice is to use IAM Identity Center (SSO) for human access rather than long-lived IAM access keys, but for programmatic access from scripts or applications, IAM roles attached to compute resources are preferred. The following commands verify a working AWS setup:

# Install the AWS CLI (macOS/Linux)
curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip" -o "awscliv2.zip"
unzip awscliv2.zip && sudo ./aws/install

# Configure credentials interactively
# Prompts for Access Key ID, Secret Access Key, region, and output format
aws configure

# Verify the configuration
aws configure list

# Confirm the authenticated identity
aws sts get-caller-identity
# Output example:
# {
#   "UserId": "AIDA...",
#   "Account": "123456789012",
#   "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/alice"
# }

# List the AWS regions available to your account
aws ec2 describe-regions --query 'Regions[*].RegionName' --output table

Once the CLI is configured, any cloud resource can be created, inspected, or destroyed through commands. The same operations are available programmatically through the AWS SDK, enabling automation, infrastructure-as-code workflows, and integration into CI/CD pipelines.
Examples
Simple: Creating and Using Cloud Storage (IaaS)

S3 is the canonical IaaS storage example. The following Python code creates a bucket, writes an object, retrieves it, and cleans up — demonstrating on-demand self-service and measured service characteristics in a few lines:

import boto3
import uuid

# Create an S3 client — demonstrates on-demand self-service:
# no human at AWS is involved in this interaction
s3 = boto3.client('s3', region_name='us-east-1')

# Use a UUID suffix to guarantee a globally unique bucket name
bucket_name = f'cloud-intro-demo-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}'

# Provision storage: the bucket is available within seconds
s3.create_bucket(Bucket=bucket_name)
print(f'Created bucket: {bucket_name}')

# Write an object — measured service: AWS bills per GB-month stored
s3.put_object(
    Bucket=bucket_name,
    Key='hello.txt',
    Body=b'Hello from the cloud!',
    ContentType='text/plain'
)
print('Uploaded object: hello.txt')

# Retrieve the object
response = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket_name, Key='hello.txt')
content = response['Body'].read().decode('utf-8')
print(f'Retrieved content: {content}')

# Release resources — measured service: billing stops when content is deleted
s3.delete_object(Bucket=bucket_name, Key='hello.txt')
s3.delete_bucket(Bucket=bucket_name)
print('Resources released — billing for this demo: fractions of a cent')
Exploring Global Reach

A defining property of cloud computing is that the provider's shared resource pool spans multiple geographic regions. The following example queries which AWS regions are available, illustrating the "broad network access" and "resource pooling" characteristics:

import boto3

ec2 = boto3.client('ec2', region_name='us-east-1')

# Query available geographic regions — no human at AWS is contacted
response = ec2.describe_regions(AllRegions=False)  # only opted-in regions
regions = sorted(r['RegionName'] for r in response['Regions'])

print(f'Your account has access to {len(regions)} AWS regions:')
for region in regions:
    print(f'  {region}')

# Regions span continents: us-east-1 (N. Virginia), eu-west-1 (Ireland),
# ap-southeast-1 (Singapore), sa-east-1 (São Paulo), etc.
# A workload can be deployed to any of these in minutes via the same API.

# Each region consists of multiple Availability Zones (isolated data centers)
az_response = ec2.describe_availability_zones(
    Filters=[{'Name': 'state', 'Values': ['available']}]
)
azs = az_response['AvailabilityZones']
print(f'\nus-east-1 has {len(azs)} Availability Zones:')
for az in azs:
    print(f'  {az["ZoneName"]} — {az["ZoneId"]}')
Real-World Example

Consider an e-commerce platform processing normal daily traffic through a cluster of EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. On Black Friday the traffic pattern changes dramatically: order volume increases 20x over baseline within 30 minutes of midnight. On-premises, this platform would require permanent hardware that sits 95% idle for 51 weeks of the year — an enormous capital waste. In AWS, an Auto Scaling group monitors CPU utilization and adds EC2 instances automatically as traffic grows, then terminates them as traffic subsides over the following days.

import boto3

autoscaling = boto3.client('autoscaling', region_name='us-east-1')

# Create an Auto Scaling group: min 2 instances at all times,
# scale up to 40 during peak events, maintain 6 for normal load
response = autoscaling.create_auto_scaling_group(
    AutoScalingGroupName='ecommerce-web-asg',
    LaunchTemplate={
        'LaunchTemplateId': 'lt-0abcd1234efgh5678',  # pre-defined template
        'Version': '$Latest'
    },
    MinSize=2,          # always on: 2 instances
    MaxSize=40,         # peak capacity: 40 instances
    DesiredCapacity=6,  # normal steady state
    AvailabilityZones=['us-east-1a', 'us-east-1b', 'us-east-1c'],
    TargetGroupARNs=['arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/web/abc123'],
    Tags=[{'Key': 'Environment', 'Value': 'production', 'PropagateAtLaunch': True}]
)

# Configure scaling policy: add instances when average CPU > 70%
autoscaling.put_scaling_policy(
    AutoScalingGroupName='ecommerce-web-asg',
    PolicyName='scale-on-cpu',
    PolicyType='TargetTrackingScaling',
    TargetTrackingConfiguration={
        'PredefinedMetricSpecification': {
            'PredefinedMetricType': 'ASGAverageCPUUtilization'
        },
        'TargetValue': 70.0  # maintain ~70% CPU utilization
    }
)

print('Auto Scaling group configured.')
print('During Black Friday, AWS will provision up to 40 instances automatically.')
print('Billing accrues only for the hours those extra instances actually run.')

The retailer pays for 40 instances only during the hours they run. The rest of the year, billing reflects the baseline 6 instances. On-premises, buying hardware for the peak load would mean owning 40 servers, paying for their power, cooling, and rack space continuously, and decommissioning them on a depreciation schedule regardless of whether they are used.
Common Mistakes
Lift-and-shift without re-architecting.

Moving an on-premises application to cloud VMs without redesigning for elasticity or managed services captures few of the cloud's benefits. The application still behaves as if it is on fixed hardware; costs are often higher than on-premises without the operational flexibility that justifies cloud adoption.
Ignoring cost optimization from day one.

It is easy to leave resources running after they are no longer needed. Unattached EBS volumes, idle EC2 instances, and forgotten load balancers all accrue billing. Tag resources from the start, enable billing alerts, and use AWS Cost Explorer weekly.
Misunderstanding the shared responsibility model.

AWS secures the cloud infrastructure (physical hardware, hypervisor, network). The customer is responsible for security in the cloud: IAM permissions, data encryption, VPC configuration, OS patching, and application security. Assuming AWS handles all security is a major mistake.
Storing credentials in code or environment files.

Hardcoding AWS access keys in source code or committing .env files to version control exposes credentials that can be scraped and used to run up large AWS bills. Use IAM roles for compute resources and AWS Secrets Manager for application secrets.
Running everything in a single Availability Zone.
A single AZ is a single physical data center. Outages, though rare, do occur. Production workloads should span at least two AZs to survive a data center failure without downtime.
Using cloud as a substitute for architecture decisions.

Vertical scaling (moving to a larger instance type) delays addressing architectural bottlenecks but does not eliminate them. Stateful monoliths, single points of failure, and unbounded database queries become more expensive in cloud, not less.
Best Practices
Use IAM roles for all compute resources instead of long-lived access keys. Roles issue temporary credentials automatically and rotate them without any application code changes.
Enable CloudTrail in all regions from day one. CloudTrail records every API call made in your account, providing an audit log that is essential for security incident response and compliance.

Apply the principle of least privilege to all IAM policies. Grant only the permissions required for a specific task, not broad administrator access. Use IAM Access Analyzer to identify overly permissive policies.
Tag every resource with at minimum: environment (dev/staging/prod), team, and project. Tags enable cost allocation, automated cleanup scripts, and policy enforcement.

Prefer managed services (RDS over self-hosted MySQL, ElastiCache over self-hosted Redis) unless there is a specific reason that the managed service cannot meet requirements. Managed services reduce patching, backup, and failover burden significantly.

Design for failure from the start. Assume any instance, AZ, or service can fail at any time and architect so the application continues operating. AWS Well-Architected Framework's reliability pillar provides detailed guidance.

Use Infrastructure as Code (CloudFormation, CDK, Terraform) to provision all resources. IaC makes infrastructure reproducible, reviewable in version control, and deployable consistently across environments.
Performance and Scalability

The fundamental scalability advantage of cloud is horizontal scaling: adding more instances rather than upgrading to larger hardware. Horizontal scaling is effectively unlimited in cloud (to practical account limits), whereas vertical scaling hits physical hardware maximums. A web application that cannot serve traffic from a single server can be placed behind a load balancer with an Auto Scaling group; adding instances takes under two minutes and the process is fully automated.

AWS provides explicit SLAs for its compute services. EC2 carries a 99.99% monthly uptime SLA per region (roughly 52 minutes of downtime per year), achievable when workloads span multiple Availability Zones. Amazon S3 is designed for 99.999999999% (eleven nines) object durability, achieved by automatically replicating data across a minimum of three physically separated AZs within a region. These durability levels are practically impossible to replicate in a small on-premises data center.

For latency-sensitive workloads, AWS CloudFront distributes content from 450+ Points of Presence worldwide, reducing the distance between users and cached content. For database read scalability, RDS Read Replicas and ElastiCache allow read traffic to be distributed without changing application architecture. The key principle is that cloud infrastructure provides the building blocks; the application must still be designed to use them effectively.
Tooling and Ecosystem
AWS Management Console.
A web-based GUI for browsing, creating, and managing all AWS resources. Appropriate for exploration and one-off administrative tasks. Not suitable for automation or repeatable deployments.
AWS CLI.
Command-line interface for all AWS services. Scriptable in shell, suitable for manual operations and simple automation. Installed with
pip install awscli
or downloaded directly from AWS.
AWS SDK (boto3 for Python).
Language-native libraries for programmatic access to all AWS services. boto3 is the Python SDK;
@aws-sdk/client-*
packages serve JavaScript/TypeScript, and AWS provides SDKs for Java, Go, Rust, .NET, Ruby, and PHP.
AWS CDK and CloudFormation.

Infrastructure as Code tools. CloudFormation uses YAML/JSON templates; CDK lets developers write infrastructure in TypeScript, Python, or Java and synthesizes CloudFormation. Both enable repeatable, version-controlled infrastructure definitions.
Terraform.
A provider-agnostic IaC tool by HashiCorp with a large AWS provider. Many organizations prefer Terraform because it supports AWS, Azure, GCP, and on-premises systems from a single toolchain.
AWS Cost Explorer and AWS Budgets.

Cost Explorer provides historical cost analysis and forecasts. Budgets allows setting spending thresholds with SNS or email alerts when costs approach or exceed the defined threshold — essential for preventing billing surprises.
AWS Trusted Advisor.

Automated checks across cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits. Highlights idle resources, security groups with open access, and under-utilized reserved instances.
Comparison with Alternatives
Aspect
On-Premises
Colocation (Colo)
Public Cloud (AWS)
Initial cost
High (hardware CapEx)
High (hardware CapEx + colo fees)
Zero (pay-as-you-go)
Provisioning time
Weeks to months
Weeks to months
Seconds to minutes
Elasticity
None
Limited (buy more hardware)
Unlimited (auto-scaling)
Geographic reach
Single site
1–few sites
30+ regions globally
Hardware control
Full
Full (your hardware)
Limited (Dedicated Hosts available)
Ops burden
Highest
High (manage hardware)
Lowest with managed services
Best for
Highly sensitive data, air-gapped systems
Predictable workloads, data sovereignty
Variable workloads, new projects, global scale
Security and Reliability

AWS operates under a shared responsibility model. AWS is responsible for security of the cloud — the physical security of data centers, the hypervisor, the global network backbone, and the hardware on which services run. Customers are responsible for security in the cloud — the configuration of the services they use, the access controls they apply, the encryption of their data, and the security of their application code.

This distinction is often misunderstood. Storing data in S3 does not automatically encrypt it — the customer must enable server-side encryption. Launching an EC2 instance into a public subnet with an unrestricted security group exposes it to the internet — the customer is responsible for configuring the security group and VPC correctly. AWS provides all the tools to secure a workload properly; applying them is the customer's responsibility.
Enable server-side encryption on all S3 buckets (AES-256 or KMS) and EBS volumes. AWS does not encrypt data at rest by default on all services.
Block public access on S3 buckets at the account level unless a specific bucket needs public content. Unintentional public S3 buckets have caused significant data breaches.
Enable AWS GuardDuty for automated threat detection. GuardDuty analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to detect credential compromise, unusual API calls, and lateral movement.

Use VPC security groups as virtual firewalls at the instance level and Network ACLs at the subnet level. Apply the principle of least privilege: allow only the specific ports and source IPs that each service requires.
Enable Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on the root account and all privileged IAM users immediately. Root account credentials should never be used for day-to-day operations.
Mental Model

The most durable mental model for cloud computing is the electricity utility analogy. Before commercial power grids, factories built and operated their own generators. This was expensive, required specialized engineering staff, and delivered power only in the quantities for which the generator was sized — not more, not less. When electric utilities emerged, factories stopped generating power and started buying it. They could consume as much electricity as they needed, scale up instantly by plugging in more machines, and pay only for what they used.

Cloud computing is to computing what the electrical grid was to manufacturing. Organizations no longer need to own and operate their own computing infrastructure any more than they need to own a power plant to run their office lights. They consume computing as a utility: on demand, measured, and billed for what is actually used.

The analogy breaks down in one important respect: unlike electricity, cloud services expose rich programmable APIs. The cloud is not just a commodity resource — it is an ecosystem of several hundred managed services that can replace large engineering efforts. A team does not just "rent servers"; it uses managed databases, AI inference endpoints, streaming data pipelines, and globally distributed content delivery, all accessible through the same API-driven model.
Key Takeaways

Cloud computing delivers shared, on-demand, metered computing resources over the internet. The NIST definition identifies five essential characteristics: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service.

The three service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — differ in how responsibility is divided between the customer and provider. IaaS gives the most control; SaaS gives the least. Most real-world AWS architectures combine all three.

The primary economic shift is from capital expenditure (buying hardware) to operational expenditure (paying for usage). This eliminates upfront investment, reduces waste from idle hardware, and aligns IT spending directly with business activity.

Rapid elasticity is the cloud's most transformative capability for applications with variable demand. Auto Scaling groups can provision tens of additional instances in minutes and release them just as quickly, paying only for the time they actually run.

AWS operates under a shared responsibility model: AWS secures the infrastructure; customers are responsible for configuring what they deploy on it correctly. Security groups, IAM policies, encryption, and CloudTrail are customer responsibilities.

Cloud is not universally cheaper than on-premises. The break-even point depends on utilization. Variable workloads, new projects, and globally distributed applications almost always benefit from cloud. Sustained, predictable, high-utilization workloads require careful cost modeling.

The most important early practices are: use IAM roles instead of access keys, enable CloudTrail and MFA on day one, tag all resources, and use Infrastructure as Code from the start. These decisions are much harder to retrofit than to establish at the beginning.
